forecasts.reasoning:
John Morgan has publicly teased a run for Florida Governor and recently began forming a new political party to support a potential bid. While he faces an early-to-mid June 2025 deadline to change his party affiliation in order to run on a new party's ticket (due to Florida's 365-day affiliation requirement), this only dictates when he must update his voter registration, not when he must announce his candidacy. 

As of mid-to-late May 2025, Morgan stated he is waiting to see how the gubernatorial field shapes up before deciding whether to run, specifically waiting to see if any candidates emerge whom he "can't tolerate." Because several prominent politicians (like Sen. Jason Pizzo) are not expected to formally announce their campaigns until fall 2025, it is highly unlikely Morgan would prematurely declare his candidacy in June. Morgan, who is drawn to free PR, has every incentive to keep the speculation alive and delay an official announcement. With the July 1, 2025, deadline less than a month away from the Jun 6 cutoff, and no concrete campaign rollout scheduled, my estimate is quite low.

The knowledge cutoff is June 6, 2025, and the resolution date is July 1, 2025, leaving a window of only 25 days for the contract to be cancelled. Recent parliamentary hearings in May 2025 emphasized that the contract contains no standard termination clause, and a unilateral cancellation by Bulgaria would trigger a massive structural penalty of BGN 4.2 billion. Consequently, Bulgaria has been trying to renegotiate the contract rather than cancel it outright. 

On the other hand, Turkey has an enormously lucrative contract and has entirely rejected previous proposals to cancel or alter it. While Bulgargaz is heavily in arrears (approx. BGN 300 million as of May 2025) and has suspended some payments, BOTAŞ is disincentivized from cancelling the contract itself, as doing so might jeopardize its claims to future guaranteed payments beneath the take-or-pay agreement; it would far more likely pursue international arbitration. Because of the massive financial penalties preventing Bulgaria from cancelling and Turkey wanting to keep its advantageous deal intact, there is a very low chance of cancellation in the extremely short remaining 25-day timeframe.

The knowledge cutoff is June 6, 2025, and the resolution date is July 1, 2025, leaving less than a month for ratification. As of recent 2024 and early 2025 reports, Sudan remains one of the few signatory nations (along with Libya, Somalia, South Sudan, etc.) that have not yet ratified the AfCFTA agreement. Crucially, Sudan has been embroiled in a devastating civil war since April 2023 between the SAF and RSF, crippling normal government functions, and the country has been suspended from the African Union since the military coup in October 2021. With no evidence of recent legislative or executive action to ratify the agreement, severe administrative disruptions, and merely 25 days remaining before the resolution deadline, the likelihood of a sudden ratification is extremely low. Therefore, the probability is set to the minimum allowed bound of 5%.
